note.soap:
S:
35-year-old patient.
Patient is not pregnant.
O: Not applicable for phone encounter.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease, suggested by bullseye rash (erythema migrans) and tick exposure in an endemic area.  
  - Differential:  
    1. Early Lyme disease  
    2. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
    3. Cellulitis or local skin infection  
    4. Other tick-borne illnesses (less likely without systemic symptoms)  
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms such as fever, joint pain, neurological symptoms, or carditis signs reported. No signs of severe allergic reaction or anaphylaxis.

P:  
  - Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended due to presence of bullseye rash and tick exposure >36 hours in an endemic area.  
  - Plan: Advise patient to seek prompt medical evaluation for clinical examination and initiation of appropriate antibiotic therapy for Lyme disease. Educate on signs of progression including fever, joint swelling, neurological symptoms.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek emergency care if fever, worsening rash, neurological symptoms (e.g., facial palsy, severe headache), or cardiac symptoms develop. Advise to monitor rash size and systemic symptoms closely.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Bullseye rash (erythema migrans) is diagnostic of early Lyme disease per CDC guidance.  
- Tick attachment >36 hours increases risk of Lyme transmission.  
- Early antibiotic treatment reduces risk of disseminated disease (CDC Lyme disease guidelines).
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
